title: Proper base change for zero cycles
speaker: Moritz Kerz
affiliation: University of Regensburg; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: We study the restriction map to the closed fiber for the Chow group of zero-cycles over a complete discrete valuation ring. It turns out that, for proper families of varieties and for certain finite coefficients, the restriction map is an isomorphism. One can also ask whether for other motivic cohomology groups with finite coefficients one gets a restriction isomorphism.

proof_stages.label: Semistable DVR setup and motivic restriction question
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes a projective flat regular model over a DVR with simple-normal-crossing reduced special fiber, finite coefficients prime to the residue characteristic, recalls CDH motivic cohomology, and asks when restriction from the model to the special fiber is an isomorphism.

proof_stages.label: Failure in general and zero-cycle conjectural range
proof_stages.summary: Negative and positive known cases show that arbitrary motivic proper base change is too optimistic but motivate a finite-coefficient conjecture in the motivic degrees governed by zero cycles, with the talk focusing on the case H^{2d,d} where the source is CH_1(x).

proof_stages.label: Presentation of C(y) by smooth zero-cycles and relations
proof_stages.summary: The special-fiber group C(y)=H^{2d,d}(y) is presented as zero-cycles on the smooth locus of the SNC variety y modulo two explicit kinds of rational-function relations, one internal to curves away from the singular locus and one connecting components along SNC curves.

proof_stages.label: Good-position cycles, moving lemma, and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: Using the Gabber–Liu–Lorenzini moving lemma, good-position flat one-cycles compute CH_1(x), the intersection map to zero-cycles on y_sm is defined, and the main theorem is stated: whenever the compatible specialization map exists, it is uniquely an isomorphism, with several unconditional cases and corollaries listed.

proof_stages.label: Surjective lifting of smooth special zero-cycles
proof_stages.summary: To build an inverse, each closed point of the smooth locus of y is lifted by choosing local parameters and lifting them to x, producing a finite flat regular one-cycle meeting y transversally and proving the preliminary restriction map is surjective.

proof_stages.label: Independence of lifts via dimension-one reduction
proof_stages.summary: The image of the kernel of the preliminary restriction map is shown to vanish in CH_1(x): first in relative dimension one using Picard groups, étale cohomology, and proper base change, then in higher dimension by Bertini hypersurfaces that reduce controlled differences of cycles to the dimension-one case.

proof_stages.label: Descent through the motivic relations
proof_stages.summary: The candidate inverse from smooth-locus zero-cycles is proved to annihilate the relation group R in the presentation of C(y), by lifting the defining curves, functions, and divisors to x, so it factors through C(y) and completes the isomorphism proof.

proof_stages.label: p-primary nonreduced-fiber outlook
proof_stages.summary: For coefficients divisible by the residue characteristic, the speaker proposes replacing the reduced special fiber by nonreduced thickenings and Milnor K-cohomology, conjectures a pro-isomorphism for the resulting restriction maps, and relates this to expected structure theorems for CH_0 over p-adic fields.

examples.name: Rosenschon–Srinivas elliptic-curve product counterexample
examples.note: A product of three copies of a suitable elliptic curve over a p-adic field has infinite finite-coefficient CH_1, while the corresponding special-fiber motivic group is expected finite, showing that unrestricted motivic proper base change cannot be injective and motivating the zero-cycle restrictions.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the elliptic-curve product data to trace the relevant motivic/Chow maps and argue from an infinite generic group versus expected finite special-fiber group that specialization cannot be injective

examples.name: Kollár specialization for separably rationally connected fibers
examples.note: For a smooth family with separably rationally connected special fiber, Kollár's integral specialization isomorphism for CH_0 is cited as a positive case suggesting that zero-cycle base change may hold in special ranges.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker states Kollár’s specialization theorem for smooth families with separably rationally connected special fiber and later cites it as a comparison, without working through a concrete family or proof

passes.reason: Separably rationally connected fibers form a broad systematic positive class for which Kollár proves specialization is an isomorphism.

examples.name: Sato–Bloch finite-coefficient zero-cycle case
examples.note: When the residue field is finite or algebraically closed, the CH_1 cycle map to étale homology and étale proper base change give a known positive finite-coefficient case, serving as evidence for the conjectural zero-cycle statement.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker lays out the Sato–Bloch zero-cycle setup via the cycle map to étale homology, applies proper base change and the known cycle-map isomorphism, and derives the resulting motivic isomorphism

passes.reason: The finite-coefficient zero-cycle/top-degree Chow case lies at the minimal-dimensional boundary of cycle theory and is used as a positive proper-base-change case.
The Sato–Bloch result concerns the zero-cycle, hence minimal-dimensional cycle, case and is used as a positive finite-coefficient base-change example.

The Sato–Bloch theorem treats top-codimension zero cycles with finite coefficients, an endpoint case used as positive evidence.
